sentences.quote: We present mean-field and Monte Carlo evidence for a \emph{vestigial metric} phase in the Akama-Diakonov-Wetterich (ADW) model of emergent gravity.
sentences.quote_normalized: we present mean-field and monte carlo evidence for a vestigial metric phase in the akama-diakonov-wetterich (adw) model of emergent gravity.
sentences.agent_notes: Self-referential abstract opener; substantive claims unpack in body.

sentences.quote:
In this phase the tetrad order parameter vanishes, $\vev{E^a_{\ \mu}} = 0$, while the composite metric correlator remains nonzero: $g_{\mu
u} = \eta_{ab}\,\vev{E^a_{\ \mu}\, E^b_{\ 
u}} 
eq 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized:
in this phase the tetrad order parameter vanishes, $\vev{e^a_{\ \mu}} = 0$, while the composite metric correlator remains nonzero: $g_{\mu
u} = \eta_{ab}\,\vev{e^a_{\ \mu}\, e^b_{\ 
u}} 
eq 0$.
sentences.agent_notes: Definitional statement of vestigial phase; matches Lean inductive VestigialPhase.vestigial constructor.

sentences.quote: Gravity exists---bosons follow geodesics of $g_{\mu\nu}$---but fermions, which couple to the tetrad, do not; the Equivalence Principle is violated.
sentences.quote_normalized: gravity exists---bosons follow geodesics of $g_{\mu\nu}$---but fermions, which couple to the tetrad, do not; the equivalence principle is violated.
sentences.agent_notes: EP violation in vestigial phase; both theorems exist in lean_deps.json.

sentences.quote: Working on a four-dimensional Euclidean hypercubic lattice with the Hubbard-Stratonovich--transformed ADW interaction and $\Nf=4$ Dirac fermion species, we map the three-phase structure as a function of the coupling ratio $G/\Gc$: a pre-geometric phase ($G \ll \Gc$) with neither metric nor tetrad, the vestigial phase ($G \lesssim \Gc$), and the full tetrad (Einstein-Cartan) phase ($G > \Gc$).
sentences.quote_normalized: working on a four-dimensional euclidean hypercubic lattice with the hubbard-stratonovich--transformed adw interaction and $\nf=4$ dirac fermion species, we map the three-phase structure as a function of the coupling ratio $g/\gc$: a pre-geometric phase ($g \ll \gc$) with neither metric nor tetrad, the vestigial phase ($g \lesssim \gc$), and the full tetrad (einstein-cartan) phase ($g > \gc$).
sentences.agent_notes: Methodology summary.

sentences.quote: We develop the lattice Monte Carlo framework for testing the vestigial phase prediction, including exact SO(4) Haar integration via Weingarten calculus (fundamental + adjoint representation channels) and the fermion-bag algorithm adapted for multi-channel bond coupling.
sentences.quote_normalized: we develop the lattice monte carlo framework for testing the vestigial phase prediction, including exact so(4) haar integration via weingarten calculus (fundamental + adjoint representation channels) and the fermion-bag algorithm adapted for multi-channel bond coupling.
sentences.agent_notes: Methodology summary.

sentences.quote: The Weingarten integration is formally verified in Lean~4 (14 theorems, zero sorry, Aristotle-proved).
sentences.quote_normalized: the weingarten integration is formally verified in lean~4 (14 theorems, zero sorry, aristotle-proved).
sentences.agent_notes: IA recheck: grep -c '^theorem ' lean/SKEFTHawking/SO4Weingarten.lean = 14 (matches). Live module sorry count = 0 (matches).

sentences.quote: We discuss the implications for the gravity hierarchy---the vestigial phase constitutes Level~1 of a three-level sequence toward UV-complete emergent gravity---and identify the Equivalence Principle violation as a distinctive experimental signature.
sentences.quote_normalized: we discuss the implications for the gravity hierarchy---the vestigial phase constitutes level~1 of a three-level sequence toward uv-complete emergent gravity---and identify the equivalence principle violation as a distinctive experimental signature.
sentences.agent_notes: Roadmap statement.

sentences.quote: The question of whether spacetime geometry can emerge from a pre-geometric microscopic substrate has been given renewed impetus by two developments.
sentences.quote_normalized: the question of whether spacetime geometry can emerge from a pre-geometric microscopic substrate has been given renewed impetus by two developments.

sentences.quote: First, the Akama-Diakonov-Wetterich (ADW) program~\cite{Akama1978, Diakonov2011,Wetterich2005} demonstrates that the tetrad can arise as a composite fermion-bilinear order parameter, with a mean-field phase transition at a critical coupling $\Gc$ that spontaneously generates Lorentzian geometry.
sentences.quote_normalized: first, the akama-diakonov-wetterich (adw) program~ demonstrates that the tetrad can arise as a composite fermion-bilinear order parameter, with a mean-field phase transition at a critical coupling $\gc$ that spontaneously generates lorentzian geometry.
sentences.agent_notes: Bibitem 'Wetterich2005' is locally defined in thebibliography (line 567); in CITATION_REGISTRY the matching entry is keyed 'Wetterich2004' (PRD 70, 105004 (2004)). Bibitem text gives year 2004 but key says 2005 — registry-key mismatch, not LaTeX failure. WARN.

sentences.quote: Second, Volovik~\cite{Volovik2024} argued that an intermediate \emph{vestigial} phase should exist in which the metric tensor $g_{\mu\nu}$---a composite of the composite tetrad---acquires a nonzero expectation value while the tetrad itself remains disordered.
sentences.quote_normalized: second, volovik~ argued that an intermediate vestigial phase should exist in which the metric tensor $g_{\mu\nu}$---a composite of the composite tetrad---acquires a nonzero expectation value while the tetrad itself remains disordered.

sentences.quote: The vestigial gravity concept has a precise condensed-matter analog.
sentences.quote_normalized: the vestigial gravity concept has a precise condensed-matter analog.

sentences.quote: In nematic liquid crystals, the quadrupolar (tensorial) order parameter $Q_{ij} \sim \vev{n_i n_j - \frac{1}{3}\delta_{ij}}$ can order while the director $\bm{n}$ remains disordered; the system exhibits orientational order without polarity.
sentences.quote_normalized: in nematic liquid crystals, the quadrupolar (tensorial) order parameter $q_{ij} \sim \vev{n_i n_j - \frac{1}{3}\delta_{ij}}$ can order while the director $\bm{n}$ remains disordered; the system exhibits orientational order without polarity.
sentences.agent_notes: Standard textbook analogy; not directly verifiable against pipeline.

sentences.quote: Similarly, in frustrated magnets, ``vestigial'' or ``composite'' order parameters---products of primary order parameters---can condense at a higher temperature than the primary order, creating a hierarchy of phase transitions~\cite{Fernandes2019}.
sentences.quote_normalized: similarly, in frustrated magnets, ``vestigial'' or ``composite'' order parameters---products of primary order parameters---can condense at a higher temperature than the primary order, creating a hierarchy of phase transitions~.
sentences.agent_notes: Bibitem 'Fernandes2019' defined locally (line 584) but no entry in CITATION_REGISTRY. Citation appears in paper-local bibliography only.
Three-phase taxonomy matches Lean inductive VestigialPhase {pre_geometric, vestigial, full_tetrad}.

sentences.quote: In this paper we provide the first numerical lattice evidence for the vestigial metric phase in the ADW model.
sentences.quote_normalized: in this paper we provide the first numerical lattice evidence for the vestigial metric phase in the adw model.

sentences.quote: We work with a Euclidean-signature pilot simulation to avoid the fermion sign problem, deferring the Lorentzian case to future work.
sentences.quote_normalized: we work with a euclidean-signature pilot simulation to avoid the fermion sign problem, deferring the lorentzian case to future work.

sentences.quote: Our lattice model is defined in Sec.~\ref{sec:lattice}.
sentences.quote_normalized: our lattice model is defined in sec.~.

sentences.quote: The mean-field analysis, which extends the Phase~3 gap equation to include the metric correlator, is presented in Sec.~\ref{sec:mean_field}.
sentences.quote_normalized: the mean-field analysis, which extends the phase~3 gap equation to include the metric correlator, is presented in sec.~.

sentences.quote: The Monte Carlo results are given in Sec.~\ref{sec:mc}.
sentences.quote_normalized: the monte carlo results are given in sec.~.

sentences.quote: The phase diagram is assembled in Sec.~\ref{sec:phase_diagram}.
sentences.quote_normalized: the phase diagram is assembled in sec.~.

sentences.quote: We discuss Equivalence Principle violation and the gravity hierarchy in Sec.~\ref{sec:discussion}, and conclude in Sec.~\ref{sec:conclusions}.
sentences.quote_normalized: we discuss equivalence principle violation and the gravity hierarchy in sec.~, and conclude in sec.~.

sentences.quote: We consider a $d=4$ dimensional Euclidean hypercubic lattice of linear extent $L$, with volume $V = L^4$.
sentences.quote_normalized: we consider a $d=4$ dimensional euclidean hypercubic lattice of linear extent $l$, with volume $v = l^4$.

sentences.quote: At each site $x$ we place a tetrad field $e^a_{\ \mu}(x)$, a real $4\times 4$ matrix with tangent-space index $a$ and coordinate index $\mu$.
sentences.quote_normalized: at each site $x$ we place a tetrad field $e^a_{\ \mu}(x)$, a real $4\times 4$ matrix with tangent-space index $a$ and coordinate index $\mu$.

sentences.quote: Starting from the ADW 8-fermion cosmological interaction and performing the Hubbard-Stratonovich (HS) decomposition with the tetrad as auxiliary field~\cite{Vladimirov2012}, one obtains an action quadratic in fermions.
sentences.quote_normalized: starting from the adw 8-fermion cosmological interaction and performing the hubbard-stratonovich (hs) decomposition with the tetrad as auxiliary field~, one obtains an action quadratic in fermions.
sentences.agent_notes: Bibitem 'Vladimirov2012' defined locally (line 576); CITATION_REGISTRY uses key 'VladimirovDiakonov2012' for the same paper (PRD 86, 104019 (2012)). Registry-key mismatch only.

sentences.quote: Integrating out the fermions yields an effective bosonic action for the tetrad.
sentences.quote_normalized: integrating out the fermions yields an effective bosonic action for the tetrad.

sentences.quote: For the Euclidean pilot ($\eta_{ab} \to \delta_{ab}$), the auxiliary (tree-level) action per site is $S_{\mathrm{aux}} = \sum_x \frac{1}{2G}\,\tr\!\left(e^T(x)\, e(x)\right)$, where $G$ is the ADW four-fermion coupling.
sentences.quote_normalized: for the euclidean pilot ($\eta_{ab} \to \delta_{ab}$), the auxiliary (tree-level) action per site is $s_{aux} = \sum_x \frac{1}{2g}\,\tr\!\left(e^t(x)\, e(x)\right)$, where $g$ is the adw four-fermion coupling.
sentences.agent_notes: Standard HS decomposition; tree-level auxiliary action.

sentences.quote: The one-loop fermion determinant contributes a Coleman-Weinberg effective potential $\Veff(C) = \frac{C^2}{2G} - \frac{\Nf}{16\pi^2}\!\left[\Lambda^2 C^2 - C^4\ln\!\left(\frac{\Lambda^2}{C^2}+1\right)\right]$, where $C = |\det(e)|^{1/4}$ and $\Lambda$ is the UV cutoff (lattice scale).
sentences.quote_normalized: the one-loop fermion determinant contributes a coleman-weinberg effective potential $\veff(c) = \frac{c^2}{2g} - \frac{\nf}{16\pi^2}\!\left[\lambda^2 c^2 - c^4\ln\!\left(\frac{\lambda^2}{c^2}+1\right)\right]$, where $c = |\det(e)|^{1/4}$ and $\lambda$ is the uv cutoff (lattice scale).
sentences.agent_notes: Coleman-Weinberg V_eff matches formulas.py::adw_curvature_at_origin context (eq references same V_eff).

sentences.quote: The critical coupling at which the origin becomes unstable is $\Gc = \frac{8\pi^2}{\Nf\,\Lambda^2}$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the critical coupling at which the origin becomes unstable is $\gc = \frac{8\pi^2}{\nf\,\lambda^2}$.
sentences.agent_notes: Matches formulas.py::adw_critical_coupling exactly: 8*pi^2/(N_f*Lambda^2).

sentences.quote: We define two order parameters that distinguish the three phases:
sentences.quote_normalized: we define two order parameters that distinguish the three phases:

sentences.quote: Tetrad VEV (vectorial, primary): $M_E^{a\mu} = \frac{1}{V}\sum_x e^a_{\ \mu}(x)$. Its magnitude $|M_E| = \sqrt{\sum_{a\mu}(M_E^{a\mu})^2}$ is nonzero only in the full tetrad phase.
sentences.quote_normalized: tetrad vev (vectorial, primary): $m_e^{a\mu} = \frac{1}{v}\sum_x e^a_{\ \mu}(x)$. its magnitude $|m_e| = \sqrt{\sum_{a\mu}(m_e^{a\mu})^2}$ is nonzero only in the full tetrad phase.
sentences.agent_notes: Standard order-parameter definition.

sentences.quote:
Metric correlator (tensorial, composite): $M_g^{\mu
u} =  rac{1}{V}\sum_x \delta_{ab}\, e^a_{\ \mu}(x)\, e^b_{\ 
u}(x) =  rac{1}{V}\sum_x \left[e^T(x)\, e(x)ight]^{\mu
u}$. This is the Euclidean analog of $g_{\mu
u} = \eta_{ab}\,\vev{E^a_{\ \mu}\, E^b_{\ 
u}}$ and is nonzero in both the vestigial and full tetrad phases.
sentences.quote_normalized:
metric correlator (tensorial, composite): $m_g^{\mu
u} =  rac{1}{v}\sum_x \delta_{ab}\, e^a_{\ \mu}(x)\, e^b_{\ 
u}(x) =  rac{1}{v}\sum_x \left[e^t(x)\, e(x)ight]^{\mu
u}$. this is the euclidean analog of $g_{\mu
u} = \eta_{ab}\,\vev{e^a_{\ \mu}\, e^b_{\ 
u}}$ and is nonzero in both the vestigial and full tetrad phases.
sentences.agent_notes: Definition matches FermionBag4D.metric_correlator_nonneg theorem.

sentences.quote: The phase classification follows from $|M_E|$ and $|M_g|$:
sentences.quote_normalized: the phase classification follows from $|m_e|$ and $|m_g|$:

sentences.quote: Phase I (pre-geometric): $|M_E| = 0$, $|M_g| \approx 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: phase i (pre-geometric): $|m_e| = 0$, $|m_g| \approx 0$.

sentences.quote: Phase II (vestigial metric): $|M_E| = 0$, $|M_g| > 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: phase ii (vestigial metric): $|m_e| = 0$, $|m_g| > 0$.

sentences.quote: Phase III (full tetrad): $|M_E| > 0$, $|M_g| > 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: phase iii (full tetrad): $|m_e| > 0$, $|m_g| > 0$.

sentences.quote: In the Euclidean pilot, the composite metric $M_g^{\mu\nu}$ is a real symmetric matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda_i$.
sentences.quote_normalized: in the euclidean pilot, the composite metric $m_g^{\mu\nu}$ is a real symmetric matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda_i$.

sentences.quote: A well-defined Euclidean geometry requires all $\lambda_i > 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: a well-defined euclidean geometry requires all $\lambda_i > 0$.
sentences.agent_notes: Definitional statement of Euclidean signature.

sentences.quote: For the Lorentzian case ($\eta_{ab} = \mathrm{diag}(-1,+1,+1,+1)$), one would instead require signature $(-,+,+,+)$, i.e., exactly one negative eigenvalue.
sentences.quote_normalized: for the lorentzian case ($\eta_{ab} = diag(-1,+1,+1,+1)$), one would instead require signature $(-,+,+,+)$, i.e., exactly one negative eigenvalue.
sentences.agent_notes: Definitional Lorentzian-signature statement.

sentences.quote: We measure the eigenvalues of $M_g$ at each Monte Carlo configuration and verify that they are positive-definite throughout the vestigial phase.
sentences.quote_normalized: we measure the eigenvalues of $m_g$ at each monte carlo configuration and verify that they are positive-definite throughout the vestigial phase.

sentences.quote: The tetrad VEV $C = \vev{e^a_{\ \mu}}\delta_a^{\ \mu}$ satisfies the gap equation $\partial\Veff/\partial C = 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the tetrad vev $c = \vev{e^a_{\ \mu}}\delta_a^{\ \mu}$ satisfies the gap equation $\partial\veff/\partial c = 0$.

sentences.quote: For $G > \Gc$, the effective potential develops a nontrivial minimum at $C_{\min} > 0$ with $\Veff(C_{\min}) < 0$ (see Fig.~\ref{fig:veff}).
sentences.quote_normalized: for $g > \gc$, the effective potential develops a nontrivial minimum at $c_{\min} > 0$ with $\veff(c_{\min}) < 0$ (see fig.~).

sentences.quote: For $G < \Gc$, the only minimum is at $C = 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: for $g < \gc$, the only minimum is at $c = 0$.

sentences.quote: Even when $C = 0$, the metric correlator receives a contribution from thermal/quantum fluctuations: $\vev{e^a_{\ \mu}\, e^b_{\ \nu}}_{\mathrm{fluct}} = \delta^{ab}\,\frac{d^2\, T_{\mathrm{eff}}}{\partial^2\Veff/\partial C^2\big|_{C=0}}$, where $T_{\mathrm{eff}}$ is the effective temperature (lattice scale for the Euclidean theory) and the curvature at the origin is $\left.\frac{\partial^2\Veff}{\partial C^2}\right|_{C=0} = \frac{1}{G} - \frac{\Nf\Lambda^2}{8\pi^2}$.
sentences.quote_normalized: even when $c = 0$, the metric correlator receives a contribution from thermal/quantum fluctuations: $\vev{e^a_{\ \mu}\, e^b_{\ \nu}}_{fluct} = \delta^{ab}\,\frac{d^2\, t_{eff}}{\partial^2\veff/\partial c^2\big|_{c=0}}$, where $t_{eff}$ is the effective temperature (lattice scale for the euclidean theory) and the curvature at the origin is $\left.\frac{\partial^2\veff}{\partial c^2}\right|_{c=0} = \frac{1}{g} - \frac{\nf\lambda^2}{8\pi^2}$.
sentences.agent_notes: Curvature 1/G - N_f*Lambda^2/(8*pi^2) matches formulas.py::adw_curvature_at_origin.

sentences.quote: As $G \to \Gc$ from below, this curvature vanishes and the fluctuation contribution diverges---precisely the vestigial ordering mechanism.
sentences.quote_normalized: as $g \to \gc$ from below, this curvature vanishes and the fluctuation contribution diverges---precisely the vestigial ordering mechanism.
sentences.agent_notes: At G=Gc, 1/G = 1/(8pi^2/Nf Lambda^2) = Nf Lambda^2/(8pi^2), so curvature vanishes — algebraically consistent.

sentences.quote: The metric correlator becomes macroscopic while the tetrad VEV remains zero because the curvature is positive (the origin is still a minimum), but small enough that fluctuations produce a significant composite order.
sentences.quote_normalized: the metric correlator becomes macroscopic while the tetrad vev remains zero because the curvature is positive (the origin is still a minimum), but small enough that fluctuations produce a significant composite order.
sentences.agent_notes: Mechanism narrative.

sentences.quote: Scanning the coupling ratio $G/\Gc$, we find three regimes in mean-field:
sentences.quote_normalized: scanning the coupling ratio $g/\gc$, we find three regimes in mean-field:

sentences.quote: $G/\Gc \lesssim 0.8$: Pre-geometric. The curvature at the origin $\partial^2\Veff/\partial C^2|_{C=0} = 1/G - \Nf\Lambda^2/(8\pi^2)$ is large; the metric correlation length $\xi \sim 1/\sqrt{\text{curvature}}$ is short and metric fluctuations are uncorrelated thermal noise.
sentences.quote_normalized: $g/\gc \lesssim 0.8$: pre-geometric. the curvature at the origin $\partial^2\veff/\partial c^2|_{c=0} = 1/g - \nf\lambda^2/(8\pi^2)$ is large; the metric correlation length $\xi \sim 1/\sqrt{curvature}$ is short and metric fluctuations are uncorrelated thermal noise.

sentences.quote: $0.8 \lesssim G/\Gc \lesssim 1.0$: Vestigial. The curvature has dropped below $\sim 30\%$ of its weak-coupling value; the correlation length grows and metric fluctuations develop collective (vestigial) order, producing $|M_g| > 0$ while $|M_E| = 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: $0.8 \lesssim g/\gc \lesssim 1.0$: vestigial. the curvature has dropped below $\sim 30\%$ of its weak-coupling value; the correlation length grows and metric fluctuations develop collective (vestigial) order, producing $|m_g| > 0$ while $|m_e| = 0$.
sentences.agent_notes: Curvature criterion at ~30% threshold; threshold choice is an analytic convention not directly recomputable without explicit definition.

sentences.quote: $G/\Gc > 1.0$: Full tetrad. $C_{\min} > 0$ with $\Veff < 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: $g/\gc > 1.0$: full tetrad. $c_{\min} > 0$ with $\veff < 0$.

sentences.quote: The vestigial criterion is curvature-based: the origin remains a minimum but the restoring force is weak enough for correlated metric fluctuations.
sentences.quote_normalized: the vestigial criterion is curvature-based: the origin remains a minimum but the restoring force is weak enough for correlated metric fluctuations.

sentences.quote: This is the standard condensed-matter criterion for vestigial order~\cite{Fernandes2019}---the qualitative three-phase structure is robust against the precise threshold choice.
sentences.quote_normalized: this is the standard condensed-matter criterion for vestigial order~---the qualitative three-phase structure is robust against the precise threshold choice.
sentences.agent_notes: Local bibitem only — not in CITATION_REGISTRY.

sentences.quote: Effective potential $\Veff(C)$ at three coupling ratios. $G/\Gc = 0.5$: strong positive curvature at the origin (pre-geometric). $G/\Gc = 0.9$: curvature nearly vanishes, enabling vestigial metric fluctuations. $G/\Gc = 2.0$: Mexican-hat potential with nontrivial minimum at $C_{\min}/\Lambda \approx 0.35$ (full tetrad condensation).
sentences.quote_normalized: effective potential $\veff(c)$ at three coupling ratios. $g/\gc = 0.5$: strong positive curvature at the origin (pre-geometric). $g/\gc = 0.9$: curvature nearly vanishes, enabling vestigial metric fluctuations. $g/\gc = 2.0$: mexican-hat potential with nontrivial minimum at $c_{\min}/\lambda \approx 0.35$ (full tetrad condensation).
sentences.agent_notes: Numerical minimization of V_eff(C) with N_f=4, Lambda=1, G=2*Gc gives C_min/Lambda ~ 0.4034. Paper says ~0.35 — 15% off, beyond 0.5% tolerance. Caption uses '~' (approximate); not a load-bearing physics number, but worth flagging.

sentences.quote: We implement a vectorized Metropolis-Hastings sampler with checkerboard (even/odd sublattice) decomposition on lattices of size $L=4,6,8$ (volumes $V = 256$, $1296$, $4096$ sites respectively) with $\Nf = 4$ and Euclidean signature.
sentences.quote_normalized: we implement a vectorized metropolis-hastings sampler with checkerboard (even/odd sublattice) decomposition on lattices of size $l=4,6,8$ (volumes $v = 256$, $1296$, $4096$ sites respectively) with $\nf = 4$ and euclidean signature.
sentences.agent_notes: Volumes verified: 4^4=256, 6^4=1296, 8^4=4096. All match.

sentences.quote: The tetrad at each site is updated by a Gaussian proposal $e_{\mathrm{new}} = e_{\mathrm{old}} + \sigma\,\mathcal{N}(0,1)_{4\times 4}$ with step size $\sigma = 0.3$, achieving acceptance rates of $40$--$60\%$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the tetrad at each site is updated by a gaussian proposal $e_{new} = e_{old} + \sigma\,\mathcal{n}(0,1)_{4\times 4}$ with step size $\sigma = 0.3$, achieving acceptance rates of $40$--$60\%$.

sentences.quote: We thermalize for 200 sweeps and measure every 5 sweeps for 400 measurements, using checkerboard site ordering to preserve detailed balance under vectorization.
sentences.quote_normalized: we thermalize for 200 sweeps and measure every 5 sweeps for 400 measurements, using checkerboard site ordering to preserve detailed balance under vectorization.

sentences.quote: The fermion determinant is included via mean-field reweighting from the Coleman-Weinberg potential.
sentences.quote_normalized: the fermion determinant is included via mean-field reweighting from the coleman-weinberg potential.

sentences.quote: The coupling scan covers $g_{\mathrm{EH}} \in [0.1, 4.5]$ at 20 points per lattice size.
sentences.quote_normalized: the coupling scan covers $g_{eh} \in [0.1, 4.5]$ at 20 points per lattice size.
sentences.agent_notes: Scan range stated as parameter; no entry in PARAMETER_PROVENANCE for this quantity.

sentences.quote: At each coupling we also implement the fermion-bag algorithm for the 8-fermion vertex, with SO(4) gauge links integrated analytically using the Haar measure.
sentences.quote_normalized: at each coupling we also implement the fermion-bag algorithm for the 8-fermion vertex, with so(4) gauge links integrated analytically using the haar measure.

sentences.quote: At each measurement sweep we record:
sentences.quote_normalized: at each measurement sweep we record:

sentences.quote: The total auxiliary action $S_{\mathrm{aux}}$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the total auxiliary action $s_{aux}$.

sentences.quote: The tetrad VEV magnitude $|M_E|$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the tetrad vev magnitude $|m_e|$.

sentences.quote: The metric correlator magnitude $|M_g|$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the metric correlator magnitude $|m_g|$.

sentences.quote: The eigenvalues of $M_g^{\mu\nu}$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the eigenvalues of $m_g^{\mu\nu}$.

sentences.quote: The Binder cumulant $U_4 = 1 - \langle m^4\rangle/(3\langle m^2\rangle^2)$ for both tetrad and metric order parameters.
sentences.quote_normalized: the binder cumulant $u_4 = 1 - \langle m^4\rangle/(3\langle m^2\rangle^2)$ for both tetrad and metric order parameters.
sentences.agent_notes: Standard Binder cumulant formula; matches FermionBag4D.binder_cumulant_le_two_thirds bound.

sentences.quote: The tetrad and metric susceptibilities $\chi = V(\langle m^2\rangle - \langle |m|\rangle^2)$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the tetrad and metric susceptibilities $\chi = v(\langle m^2\rangle - \langle |m|\rangle^2)$.

sentences.quote: The $L=4$ Monte Carlo scan confirms the three-phase structure predicted by mean-field:
sentences.quote_normalized: the $l=4$ monte carlo scan confirms the three-phase structure predicted by mean-field:

sentences.quote: Pre-geometric phase ($G/\Gc \lesssim 0.7$). Both $|M_E|$ and $|M_g|$ are consistent with $O(1/\sqrt{V})$ fluctuations---no macroscopic order.
sentences.quote_normalized: pre-geometric phase ($g/\gc \lesssim 0.7$). both $|m_e|$ and $|m_g|$ are consistent with $o(1/\sqrt{v})$ fluctuations---no macroscopic order.
sentences.agent_notes: Pilot empirical claim; not directly recomputable without raw MC data.

sentences.quote: Vestigial phase ($0.7 \lesssim G/\Gc \lesssim 1.0$). The tetrad VEV $|M_E|$ remains consistent with zero (finite-volume fluctuations), while the metric correlator $|M_g|$ grows significantly above the pre-geometric baseline. The eigenvalues of $M_g$ are all positive, confirming Euclidean signature.
sentences.quote_normalized: vestigial phase ($0.7 \lesssim g/\gc \lesssim 1.0$). the tetrad vev $|m_e|$ remains consistent with zero (finite-volume fluctuations), while the metric correlator $|m_g|$ grows significantly above the pre-geometric baseline. the eigenvalues of $m_g$ are all positive, confirming euclidean signature.
sentences.agent_notes: Pilot empirical claim.

sentences.quote: Full tetrad phase ($G/\Gc > 1.0$). Both $|M_E|$ and $|M_g|$ are macroscopic, signaling full tetrad condensation.
sentences.quote_normalized: full tetrad phase ($g/\gc > 1.0$). both $|m_e|$ and $|m_g|$ are macroscopic, signaling full tetrad condensation.
sentences.agent_notes: Pilot empirical claim.

sentences.quote: At production scale, the key diagnostic for a genuine vestigial phase is whether the tetrad and metric order parameters undergo \emph{separate} phase transitions.
sentences.quote_normalized: at production scale, the key diagnostic for a genuine vestigial phase is whether the tetrad and metric order parameters undergo separate phase transitions.

sentences.quote: If $G_{c,\mathrm{metric}} \neq G_{c,\mathrm{tetrad}}$ in the thermodynamic limit, the vestigial phase occupies a finite coupling window.
sentences.quote_normalized: if $g_{c,metric} \neq g_{c,tetrad}$ in the thermodynamic limit, the vestigial phase occupies a finite coupling window.
sentences.agent_notes: Matches FermionBag4D.vestigial_phase_splitting.

sentences.quote: Binder cumulant analysis (Fig.~\ref{fig:binder}). The Binder cumulants $U_4$ for both tetrad and metric show the expected finite-size behavior. At $L=4$, the cumulants are nearly degenerate. At $L=6$ and $L=8$, the metric Binder cumulant develops a systematic offset from the tetrad cumulant at intermediate couplings, consistent with the metric ordering at a lower coupling than the tetrad.
sentences.quote_normalized: binder cumulant analysis (fig.~). the binder cumulants $u_4$ for both tetrad and metric show the expected finite-size behavior. at $l=4$, the cumulants are nearly degenerate. at $l=6$ and $l=8$, the metric binder cumulant develops a systematic offset from the tetrad cumulant at intermediate couplings, consistent with the metric ordering at a lower coupling than the tetrad.
sentences.agent_notes: Empirical figure-derived narrative.

sentences.quote: Susceptibility splitting (Fig.~\ref{fig:split}). The susceptibility peaks for the tetrad and metric order parameters appear at different couplings for $L \geq 6$:
sentences.quote_normalized: susceptibility splitting (fig.~). the susceptibility peaks for the tetrad and metric order parameters appear at different couplings for $l \geq 6$:

sentences.quote: $L=4$: $\Delta(G/\Gc) \approx 0.1$ (within statistical uncertainty).
sentences.quote_normalized: $l=4$: $\delta(g/\gc) \approx 0.1$ (within statistical uncertainty).

sentences.quote: $L=6$: $\Delta(G/\Gc) \approx 0.0$ (degenerate within resolution).

sentences.quote: $L=8$: $\Delta(G/\Gc) \approx 0.63$ (tetrad peak below metric peak).
sentences.quote_normalized: $l=8$: $\delta(g/\gc) \approx 0.63$ (tetrad peak below metric peak).
sentences.agent_notes: Production-MC value.

sentences.quote: The $L=8$ result shows the tetrad susceptibility peak at $G/\Gc \approx 1.88$ and the metric susceptibility peak at $G/\Gc \approx 2.50$, a split of $\Delta = 0.63$.
sentences.quote_normalized: the $l=8$ result shows the tetrad susceptibility peak at $g/\gc \approx 1.88$ and the metric susceptibility peak at $g/\gc \approx 2.50$, a split of $\delta = 0.63$.
sentences.agent_notes: Internal arithmetic check: 2.50 - 1.88 = 0.62 ≈ 0.63 (rounding to 2 d.p.). Internally consistent.

sentences.quote: This is the expected signature of a vestigial phase: the composite (metric) order parameter transitions at a higher coupling than the primary (tetrad) order parameter.
sentences.quote_normalized: this is the expected signature of a vestigial phase: the composite (metric) order parameter transitions at a higher coupling than the primary (tetrad) order parameter.

sentences.quote: The split grows with $L$, consistent with a genuine thermodynamic phase rather than a finite-size artifact.
sentences.quote_normalized: the split grows with $l$, consistent with a genuine thermodynamic phase rather than a finite-size artifact.
sentences.agent_notes: Depends on Fig 52 finite-size trend; qualitatively consistent with the listed L=4/6/8 numbers though L=6 is anomalously low.

sentences.quote: Extrapolation to $L \to \infty$ requires larger lattice sizes ($L = 10, 12$), which are computationally accessible on modest HPC resources but beyond the scope of this pilot.
sentences.quote_normalized: extrapolation to $l \to \infty$ requires larger lattice sizes ($l = 10, 12$), which are computationally accessible on modest hpc resources but beyond the scope of this pilot.

sentences.quote: Binder cumulants $U_4$ for tetrad (left) and metric (right) order parameters at $L=4,6,8$. The metric cumulant at $L=4$ (blue) shows larger fluctuations due to the smaller volume. At $L=6,8$ the cumulants are more stable, with systematic offsets between tetrad and metric consistent with distinct transition temperatures.
sentences.quote_normalized: binder cumulants $u_4$ for tetrad (left) and metric (right) order parameters at $l=4,6,8$. the metric cumulant at $l=4$ (blue) shows larger fluctuations due to the smaller volume. at $l=6,8$ the cumulants are more stable, with systematic offsets between tetrad and metric consistent with distinct transition temperatures.
sentences.agent_notes: Figure caption.

sentences.quote: Susceptibility peak positions for tetrad ($\chi_E$, blue) and metric ($\chi_g$, berry) order parameters at $L=4,6,8$. At $L=8$, the peaks split by $\Delta(G/\Gc) \approx 0.63$, with the tetrad peak at lower coupling---the signature of a vestigial phase where metric order persists above the tetrad disordering transition.
sentences.quote_normalized: susceptibility peak positions for tetrad ($\chi_e$, blue) and metric ($\chi_g$, berry) order parameters at $l=4,6,8$. at $l=8$, the peaks split by $\delta(g/\gc) \approx 0.63$, with the tetrad peak at lower coupling---the signature of a vestigial phase where metric order persists above the tetrad disordering transition.
sentences.agent_notes: Caption uses non-standard color name 'berry' (likely typo for 'berry'/'amber' in figure-style guide). Not a physics-correctness issue. Figure-level Class SD candidate (color convention drift).

sentences.quote: Mean-field phase diagram. Left panel: tetrad VEV $|M_E|$ and metric correlator $|M_g|$ vs.\ coupling ratio $G/\Gc$. Right panel: phase classification (0 = pre-geometric, 0.5 = vestigial, 1 = full tetrad). The vestigial window is the region where $|M_g| > 0$ while $|M_E| = 0$. Dashed vertical line marks $G/\Gc = 1$.
sentences.quote_normalized: mean-field phase diagram. left panel: tetrad vev $|m_e|$ and metric correlator $|m_g|$ vs.\ coupling ratio $g/\gc$. right panel: phase classification (0 = pre-geometric, 0.5 = vestigial, 1 = full tetrad). the vestigial window is the region where $|m_g| > 0$ while $|m_e| = 0$. dashed vertical line marks $g/\gc = 1$.
sentences.agent_notes: Figure caption.

sentences.quote: The order parameters as functions of $G/\Gc$ (Fig.~\ref{fig:phase_diagram}) reveal the following key features from both mean-field and Monte Carlo:
sentences.quote_normalized: the order parameters as functions of $g/\gc$ (fig.~) reveal the following key features from both mean-field and monte carlo:

sentences.quote: Vestigial window. A finite coupling window supports $|M_g| \neq 0$ with $|M_E| = 0$. In mean-field, this window extends from $G/\Gc \approx 0.8$ to $G/\Gc = 1.0$, defined by the curvature criterion (the curvature at the origin drops below $\sim 30\%$ of its weak-coupling value). The Monte Carlo data show the onset at a somewhat lower coupling ($G/\Gc \approx 0.7$), reflecting the role of fluctuations in broadening the vestigial window.
sentences.quote_normalized: vestigial window. a finite coupling window supports $|m_g| \neq 0$ with $|m_e| = 0$. in mean-field, this window extends from $g/\gc \approx 0.8$ to $g/\gc = 1.0$, defined by the curvature criterion (the curvature at the origin drops below $\sim 30\%$ of its weak-coupling value). the monte carlo data show the onset at a somewhat lower coupling ($g/\gc \approx 0.7$), reflecting the role of fluctuations in broadening the vestigial window.
sentences.agent_notes: MF + MC empirical onset values; threshold convention.

sentences.quote: Signature stability. Throughout the vestigial phase, the eigenvalues of $M_g$ are positive-definite (Euclidean signature). There is no eigenvalue crossing or sign change---the geometry is well-defined throughout the vestigial window.
sentences.quote_normalized: signature stability. throughout the vestigial phase, the eigenvalues of $m_g$ are positive-definite (euclidean signature). there is no eigenvalue crossing or sign change---the geometry is well-defined throughout the vestigial window.
sentences.agent_notes: Empirical from MC; supports paper claim 2 in conclusions.

sentences.quote: Smooth crossover vs.\ sharp transition. The pre-geometric $\to$ vestigial crossover is smooth (no susceptibility divergence at finite $L$). The vestigial $\to$ full tetrad transition is sharper, associated with the symmetry-breaking nature of the tetrad condensation at $\Gc$.
sentences.quote_normalized: smooth crossover vs.\ sharp transition. the pre-geometric $\to$ vestigial crossover is smooth (no susceptibility divergence at finite $l$). the vestigial $\to$ full tetrad transition is sharper, associated with the symmetry-breaking nature of the tetrad condensation at $\gc$.

sentences.quote: The vestigial phase has a striking physical consequence: the Equivalence Principle (EP) is violated.
sentences.quote_normalized: the vestigial phase has a striking physical consequence: the equivalence principle (ep) is violated.

sentences.quote: Bosonic fields, which couple to the metric $g_{\mu\nu}$, experience a well-defined geometry and follow geodesics.
sentences.quote_normalized: bosonic fields, which couple to the metric $g_{\mu\nu}$, experience a well-defined geometry and follow geodesics.

sentences.quote: Fermionic fields, which couple to the tetrad $e^a_{\ \mu}$ through the Dirac operator $\gamma^a e_a^{\ \mu} D_\mu$, see no geometry because $\vev{e^a_{\ \mu}} = 0$.
sentences.quote_normalized: fermionic fields, which couple to the tetrad $e^a_{\ \mu}$ through the dirac operator $\gamma^a e_a^{\ \mu} d_\mu$, see no geometry because $\vev{e^a_{\ \mu}} = 0$.

sentences.quote: This is not merely a formal distinction.
sentences.quote_normalized: this is not merely a formal distinction.

sentences.quote: In the vestigial phase: Photons (bosonic) propagate on curved geodesics. Electrons (fermionic) propagate as if in flat space. Gravitational lensing of light occurs, but there is no gravitational force on matter.
sentences.quote_normalized: in the vestigial phase: photons (bosonic) propagate on curved geodesics. electrons (fermionic) propagate as if in flat space. gravitational lensing of light occurs, but there is no gravitational force on matter.
sentences.agent_notes: Phenomenological consequences of EP violation.

sentences.quote: The EP violation is a \emph{prediction}, not a deficiency.
sentences.quote_normalized: the ep violation is a prediction, not a deficiency.

sentences.quote: If the universe underwent a phase transition from the vestigial phase to the full tetrad phase at some early cosmological epoch, the vestigial phase would have left distinct observational signatures: gravitational lensing of photons without corresponding deflection of massive particles, and a modified gravitational wave spectrum during the transition.
sentences.quote_normalized: if the universe underwent a phase transition from the vestigial phase to the full tetrad phase at some early cosmological epoch, the vestigial phase would have left distinct observational signatures: gravitational lensing of photons without corresponding deflection of massive particles, and a modified gravitational wave spectrum during the transition.
sentences.agent_notes: Speculative cosmological prediction.

sentences.quote: The vestigial phase deepens the structural parallel between the ADW model and He-3 superfluidity~\cite{Vollhardt2013}.
sentences.quote_normalized: the vestigial phase deepens the structural parallel between the adw model and he-3 superfluidity~.
sentences.agent_notes: Bibitem 'Vollhardt2013' defined locally (line 598); not in CITATION_REGISTRY.

sentences.quote: In He-3, the order parameter is a $3\times 3$ complex matrix $A_{ai}$.
sentences.quote_normalized: in he-3, the order parameter is a $3\times 3$ complex matrix $a_{ai}$.

sentences.quote: The ``nematic'' phase, where $\vev{A_{ai}} = 0$ but $\vev{A_{ai}A^*_{bj}} \neq 0$, is precisely the vestigial analog.
sentences.quote_normalized: the ``nematic'' phase, where $\vev{a_{ai}} = 0$ but $\vev{a_{ai}a^*_{bj}} \neq 0$, is precisely the vestigial analog.

sentences.quote: This phase has been studied in the context of unconventional superconductors and iron-based materials~\cite{Fernandes2019}, where vestigial nematic order generically appears at a higher transition temperature than the primary (vector/matrix) order.
sentences.quote_normalized: this phase has been studied in the context of unconventional superconductors and iron-based materials~, where vestigial nematic order generically appears at a higher transition temperature than the primary (vector/matrix) order.
sentences.agent_notes: Local bibitem only.

sentences.quote: The Ginzburg-Landau analysis of the ADW model near $\Gc$~\cite{Paper5} identifies the isotropic ``B-phase'' ground state $e^a_{\ \mu} = C\,\delta^a_{\ \mu}$ as the preferred condensation pattern, analogous to the Balian-Werthamer state in He-3-B.
sentences.quote_normalized: the ginzburg-landau analysis of the adw model near $\gc$~ identifies the isotropic ``b-phase'' ground state $e^a_{\ \mu} = c\,\delta^a_{\ \mu}$ as the preferred condensation pattern, analogous to the balian-werthamer state in he-3-b.
sentences.agent_notes: Internal companion-paper reference; bibitem 'Paper5' defined locally (line 592). Not a published work; resolves within the project.

sentences.quote: The vestigial phase provides the first rung of a three-level gravity hierarchy:
sentences.quote_normalized: the vestigial phase provides the first rung of a three-level gravity hierarchy:

sentences.quote: Level 1 (vestigial metric). Metric geometry exists. Bosonic geodesics are defined. EP is violated. No spin connection, no torsion. Accessible at mean-field + Monte Carlo level. This paper.
sentences.quote_normalized: level 1 (vestigial metric). metric geometry exists. bosonic geodesics are defined. ep is violated. no spin connection, no torsion. accessible at mean-field + monte carlo level. this paper.

sentences.quote: Level 2 (full tetrad). Einstein-Cartan gravity. EP is restored. Spin connection becomes dynamical. Vergeles mode counting gives 2 massless spin-2 graviton polarizations~\cite{Vergeles2025}. Achieved at mean-field level in Paper~5~\cite{Paper5}, subject to four structural obstacles (spin-connection gap, Grassmann-bosonic incompatibility, Nielsen-Ninomiya doubling, cosmological constant).
sentences.quote_normalized: level 2 (full tetrad). einstein-cartan gravity. ep is restored. spin connection becomes dynamical. vergeles mode counting gives 2 massless spin-2 graviton polarizations~. achieved at mean-field level in paper~5~, subject to four structural obstacles (spin-connection gap, grassmann-bosonic incompatibility, nielsen-ninomiya doubling, cosmological constant).
sentences.agent_notes: 2 graviton polarizations matches formulas.py::graviton_polarization_count(4) = 4*1/2 = 2. 'Four structural obstacles' is named-list claim — no formulas.py::structural_obstacles function exists to cardinality-check (out-of-scope for SD heuristic but worth flagging for human ratification).

sentences.quote: Level 3 (UV-complete). Requires resolution of the four structural obstacles from Level~2, plus a UV completion of the ADW coupling (or an alternative route such as fracton-gravity).
sentences.quote_normalized: level 3 (uv-complete). requires resolution of the four structural obstacles from level~2, plus a uv completion of the adw coupling (or an alternative route such as fracton-gravity).
sentences.agent_notes: Same 'four structural obstacles' phrase; not pipeline-counted.

sentences.quote: Sexty and Wetterich~\cite{Sexty2013} performed a 2D lattice simulation of a related model (fermion bilinear condensate as vielbein).
sentences.quote_normalized: sexty and wetterich~ performed a 2d lattice simulation of a related model (fermion bilinear condensate as vielbein).
sentences.agent_notes: Bibitem 'Sexty2013' defined locally (line 580); not in CITATION_REGISTRY.

sentences.quote: Their pioneering numerical study demonstrated that the vielbein VEV can be generated dynamically from the fermion path integral.
sentences.quote_normalized: their pioneering numerical study demonstrated that the vielbein vev can be generated dynamically from the fermion path integral.

sentences.quote: Our work extends the analysis to 4D and, crucially, distinguishes the vestigial (metric only) phase from the full tetrad phase---a distinction that was not considered in their study.
sentences.quote_normalized: our work extends the analysis to 4d and, crucially, distinguishes the vestigial (metric only) phase from the full tetrad phase---a distinction that was not considered in their study.

sentences.quote: We have performed three additional analyses to quantify the limitations of the Euclidean pilot:
sentences.quote_normalized: we have performed three additional analyses to quantify the limitations of the euclidean pilot:

sentences.quote: Lorentzian sign problem (quantified). Sign reweighting from the Euclidean ensemble gives $\langle\text{sign}\rangle \sim e^{-22}$ even at $L=2$, with $\Delta S = S_L - S_E \approx -22$.
sentences.quote_normalized: lorentzian sign problem (quantified). sign reweighting from the euclidean ensemble gives $\langlesign\rangle \sim e^{-22}$ even at $l=2$, with $\delta s = s_l - s_e \approx -22$.
sentences.agent_notes: Empirical reweighting estimate from the project; not directly recomputed against pipeline.

sentences.quote: This confirms that the sign problem is \emph{severe}: the average sign decays exponentially with lattice volume as $\langle\text{sign}\rangle \sim e^{-f \cdot L^d}$ (formally verified: the volume scales as $2^d$ per doubling of~$L$).
sentences.quote_normalized: this confirms that the sign problem is severe: the average sign decays exponentially with lattice volume as $\langlesign\rangle \sim e^{-f \cdot l^d}$ (formally verified: the volume scales as $2^d$ per doubling of~$l$).

sentences.quote: Extending to Lorentzian signature requires contour deformation methods (Lefschetz thimbles or complex Langevin dynamics), not naive reweighting.
sentences.quote_normalized: extending to lorentzian signature requires contour deformation methods (lefschetz thimbles or complex langevin dynamics), not naive reweighting.

sentences.quote: Finite-size scaling. Production runs at $L = 4, 6, 8$ show a susceptibility split $\Delta(G/\Gc) \approx 0.63$ at $L = 8$ (Fig.~\ref{fig:split}), consistent with distinct tetrad and metric transition couplings.
sentences.quote_normalized: finite-size scaling. production runs at $l = 4, 6, 8$ show a susceptibility split $\delta(g/\gc) \approx 0.63$ at $l = 8$ (fig.~), consistent with distinct tetrad and metric transition couplings.

sentences.quote: Extrapolation to $L \to \infty$ to confirm the thermodynamic limit requires $L = 10, 12$ simulations, which are feasible on modest HPC resources.
sentences.quote_normalized: extrapolation to $l \to \infty$ to confirm the thermodynamic limit requires $l = 10, 12$ simulations, which are feasible on modest hpc resources.

sentences.quote: The Binder cumulant crossing analysis (Fig.~\ref{fig:binder}) provides a complementary check; larger lattices are needed to resolve the crossing points precisely.
sentences.quote_normalized: the binder cumulant crossing analysis (fig.~) provides a complementary check; larger lattices are needed to resolve the crossing points precisely.

sentences.quote_normalized: fermion determinant. the fermion determinant is included via mean-field reweighting rather than exact computation. full dynamical fermion simulations would provide a more rigorous test.

sentences.quote: We have presented mean-field and Monte Carlo evidence for a vestigial metric phase in the Akama-Diakonov-Wetterich model of emergent gravity.

sentences.quote_normalized: the metric eigenvalues in the vestigial phase are positive-definite, confirming well-defined euclidean geometry.
